level-1-helm-broken-values.description: A Helm release was deployed but pods are running `nginx:latest` instead of a specific version. In the Helm chart template, the image tag is read from `values.yaml` — but the tag value was left empty, so the template resolved to `latest` as a fallback. Unpinned tags make deployments non-reproducible.
level-1-helm-broken-values.hint1: Check what image the running pod is actually using: `kubectl get pod -n k8smissions -l app=helm-app -o yaml | grep image`
level-1-helm-broken-values.hint2: You should see `image: nginx:latest`. In a Helm chart, this comes from `{{ .Values.image.tag }}` in the template. When the tag is empty or unset, the chart falls back to `latest`. Look at the manifest's `image` field — it needs a pinned version.
level-1-helm-broken-values.hint3: The container's `image` field uses the `:latest` tag. This is not a specific version — update it to a pinned tag like `nginx:1.27.4` so the deployment is reproducible and auditable.
level-1-helm-broken-values.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Helm `values.yaml` had `image.tag: ""` — an empty value. The chart template `image: {{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}` resolved to `nginx:latest`. `latest` is a moving target: it changes whenever the upstream maintainer pushes a new image, making deployments non-deterministic.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: helm-app
    image: nginx:1.27.4
```

In a real Helm workflow you would set this in `values.yaml`:
```yaml
image:
  repository: nginx
  tag: "1.27.4"
```

## Why this matters

Pinning image tags is a foundational GitOps principle: the Git repository should contain everything needed to reproduce an exact cluster state. `latest` breaks this because two identical commits can produce different running software. Tools like Renovate or Dependabot automate tag updates while keeping them explicit and reviewed.

level-2-helm-rollback.description: All pods crashed immediately after a Helm upgrade. The upgrade pushed a broken image tag (`nginx:broken-tag`) to production. In a real environment the recovery is `helm rollback` — here you need to fix the manifest directly to restore the last known good state.
level-2-helm-rollback.hint1: Check what image the failing pods are trying to use: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=webapp | grep -A3 Image`
level-2-helm-rollback.hint2: The pods are using `nginx:broken-tag` which doesn't exist in the registry. The Events section will show `ErrImagePull`. Look at the manifest's `image` field — it needs to be changed to the last working version.
level-2-helm-rollback.hint3: The container `image` field references a non-existent tag. Replace `nginx:broken-tag` with `nginx:1.27.4` to restore the working state.
level-2-helm-rollback.reveal:
## What went wrong

A Helm upgrade was run with a bad `image.tag` value — `broken-tag` doesn't exist in Docker Hub. Every pod in the Deployment enters `ImagePullBackOff` simultaneously.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: webapp
    image: nginx:1.27.4
```

In a real Helm environment, the recovery would be:
```bash
# See revision history
helm history webapp -n k8smissions

# Roll back to the last working revision
helm rollback webapp 1 -n k8smissions
```

## Why this matters

`helm rollback` is one of Helm's most valuable features. Each `helm upgrade` stores a versioned snapshot of the rendered manifests (as a Kubernetes Secret in the release namespace). A rollback atomically re-applies a previous snapshot. For zero-downtime safety, use `--atomic` on upgrades: if any pod fails to become ready within the timeout, Helm rolls back automatically without operator intervention.

level-3-kustomize-overlay.description: The staging environment is running 10 replicas — far more than appropriate for staging. The wrong Kustomize overlay was applied: production settings were pushed to the staging environment, inflating the replica count from the correct staging value of 2.
level-3-kustomize-overlay.hint1: Check how many replicas are currently running in staging: `kubectl get deployment kustomize-app -n k8smissions`
level-3-kustomize-overlay.hint2: You should see DESIRED: 10. That's a production-scale replica count running in staging. In a Kustomize overlay setup, a staging overlay would set this to a much lower value. Look at the `replicas` field in the manifest.
level-3-kustomize-overlay.hint3: The Deployment's `spec.replicas` is set to 10 — this is the production overlay value. In the staging overlay, this field should be patched to 2.
level-3-kustomize-overlay.reveal:
## What went wrong

Kustomize overlays use patches on top of a shared base. Someone applied the production overlay (`replicas: 10`) to the staging environment instead of the staging overlay (`replicas: 2`). This kind of environment mismatch is a common failure mode when promotion pipelines are manual.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  replicas: 2
```

In a Kustomize project, the staging overlay `kustomization.yaml` should have:
```yaml
patches:
- patch: |-
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    metadata:
      name: kustomize-app
    spec:
      replicas: 2
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: kustomize-app
```

## Why this matters

The Kustomize base/overlay pattern is the GitOps solution to environment drift. A base directory contains the shared configuration; each environment (staging, production) has an overlay that patches only what differs. When automated promotion pipelines apply the right overlay per environment from Git, human error in environment selection is eliminated. The risk lives at the pipeline level — the wrong overlay applied by CI is more dangerous than the wrong overlay applied by hand, because CI does it consistently.

level-4-argocd-sync.description: An ArgoCD application shows OutOfSync and Degraded status. The application is configured to deploy resources into a namespace called `webapp-prod`, but that namespace doesn't exist. Until the namespace exists, ArgoCD has nowhere to put the resources and the sync fails.
level-4-argocd-sync.hint1: Check whether the target namespace exists: `kubectl get namespace webapp-prod`
level-4-argocd-sync.hint2: The namespace doesn't exist yet. ArgoCD needs the destination namespace to be present before it can deploy into it (unless `CreateNamespace=true` is set in syncOptions). Look at what the solution manifest needs to create.
level-4-argocd-sync.hint3: The broken manifest shows `status: OutOfSync` and references `targetNamespace: webapp-prod`. You need to create that namespace and deploy a Deployment into it to bring the application to Synced state.
level-4-argocd-sync.reveal:
## What went wrong

The ArgoCD Application targets the `webapp-prod` namespace, but that namespace was never created. ArgoCD cannot deploy resources into a namespace that doesn't exist, so the application stays OutOfSync.

## Fix

Create the namespace and deploy the application:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: webapp-prod
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp-app
  namespace: webapp-prod
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp-app
        image: nginx:1.27.4
```

Also update the ConfigMap status to Synced:
```yaml
data:
  status: Synced
```

## Why this matters

In production ArgoCD setups, set `syncOptions: [CreateNamespace=true]` in the Application spec to allow ArgoCD to create the namespace automatically as part of the sync. ArgoCD's sync model compares desired state (Git) to actual state (cluster) across three dimensions: resource existence, spec content, and health status. All three must align for an application to be `Synced` and `Healthy`.

level-5-multi-env.description: Production is running, but the configuration was never promoted from development settings. The log level is DEBUG, there is only 1 replica, and there are no resource limits — all hallmarks of the dev overlay being applied to production instead of the production overlay.
level-5-multi-env.hint1: Inspect the running configuration: `kubectl get configmap env-config -n k8smissions -o yaml` and `kubectl get deployment multi-env-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -E 'replicas|limits'`
level-5-multi-env.hint2: You should see `LOG_LEVEL: debug`, `replicas: 1`, and no resource limits defined. Production needs: INFO log level, 3 replicas for availability, and resource limits to prevent noisy-neighbor impact.
level-5-multi-env.hint3: Three things are wrong at once: the ConfigMap has `LOG_LEVEL: debug` (should be `info`), the Deployment has `replicas: 1` (should be `3`), and the container has no `resources.limits` block at all.
level-5-multi-env.reveal:
## What went wrong

The dev environment config was promoted to production without being replaced by the production overlay. Three production requirements are missing:
1. Log level at `info` (not `debug`)
2. Three replicas for redundancy
3. Resource limits to protect other workloads

## Fix

```yaml
# ConfigMap
data:
  LOG_LEVEL: info
---
# Deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: multi-env-app
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 64Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 512Mi
```

## Why this matters

Config drift — where production ends up running different settings than intended — is one of the most common causes of production incidents. GitOps solves this by making Git the single source of truth: the production overlay in the repository defines exactly what production should look like, and the CD pipeline enforces it. The pattern works only when promotion means applying the correct environment-specific overlay, not manually editing live cluster state.

level-6-graceful-shutdown.description: Users experience connection reset errors during rolling updates. Pods are being killed while they still have active connections. There is nothing telling Kubernetes to wait before sending SIGTERM or to give the application time to drain — so in-flight requests are dropped the moment the pod is removed from the endpoints list.
level-6-graceful-shutdown.hint1: Look for any lifecycle configuration in the Deployment: `kubectl get deployment graceful-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A10 lifecycle`
level-6-graceful-shutdown.hint2: There are no lifecycle hooks and no `terminationGracePeriodSeconds`. When Kubernetes terminates a pod, it removes it from the Service endpoints and sends SIGTERM simultaneously — but the kube-proxy endpoint update takes a few seconds to propagate, so traffic still arrives at the dying pod.
level-6-graceful-shutdown.hint3: Two fields are missing: `lifecycle.preStop` on the container (to introduce a short delay before SIGTERM, giving kube-proxy time to update) and `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` on the pod spec (to give the app enough time to finish draining active connections).
level-6-graceful-shutdown.reveal:
## What went wrong

Kubernetes sends SIGTERM and removes the pod from endpoints at the same time. But kube-proxy (or the CNI) takes a few seconds to propagate the endpoint removal to all nodes. During that window, requests still arrive at a pod that has already received SIGTERM and may be shutting down — causing connection resets.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
  containers:
  - name: graceful-app
    image: nginx:1.27.4
    lifecycle:
      preStop:
        exec:
          command:
          - /bin/sh
          - -c
          - sleep 15
```

The `preStop` hook runs before SIGTERM is sent. The 15-second sleep gives kube-proxy time to propagate the endpoint removal before the application starts shutting down.

## Why this matters

The full pod termination sequence:
1. Pod added to `Terminating` state
2. `preStop` hook executes
3. SIGTERM sent to container
4. Container has `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` total (including preStop time) to exit cleanly
5. After grace period, SIGKILL is sent

For zero-downtime deployments: preStop sleep ≥ time for kube-proxy propagation (typically 5–15s). This pattern is essential for any production web service.

level-7-external-secrets.description: A security audit has flagged plain-text credentials committed directly to the Git repository as a Kubernetes Secret manifest. Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted — anyone with repository access can decode them. The fix is to delegate secret management to the External Secrets Operator.
level-7-external-secrets.hint1: Look at the current Secret: `kubectl get secret app-secret -n k8smissions -o yaml`. Note that `stringData` contains readable credentials that are also stored in Git.
level-7-external-secrets.hint2: Plain-text credentials in a Git-committed manifest is a critical security finding. The industry standard is to remove the credentials from Git entirely and have a secret management system (like Vault or AWS Secrets Manager) inject them at runtime via the External Secrets Operator.
level-7-external-secrets.hint3: The Secret has `stringData` with plain credential values and no annotation indicating external management. Add the `managed-by: external-secrets` annotation and update the credentials to reflect they are now managed externally.
level-7-external-secrets.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Secret manifest was committed to Git with `stringData` containing real credentials. Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded — anyone who runs `echo 'cGxhaW4tcGFzcw==' | base64 -d` gets the plaintext. In a GitOps workflow where Git is the source of truth, this means credentials are exposed to everyone with repo read access.

## Fix

Mark the Secret as externally managed:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: app-secret
  namespace: k8smissions
  annotations:
    managed-by: external-secrets
type: Opaque
stringData:
  username: app-user
  password: rotated-pass
```

In a real External Secrets Operator setup, you would replace the Secret with an `ExternalSecret` resource:
```yaml
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: app-secret
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend
    kind: SecretStore
  target:
    name: app-secret
  data:
  - secretKey: password
    remoteRef:
      key: myapp/credentials
      property: password
```

## Why this matters

Never commit Secrets to Git, even encrypted ones. The correct GitOps approach: commit only `ExternalSecret` resources (which reference, not contain, the credential), and let the External Secrets Operator sync the actual values from a secure backend (Vault, AWS SSM, GCP Secret Manager). Credentials in a secure store can be rotated without a Git commit, audit-logged, and access-controlled per team.

level-8-autoscaler.description: The HPA has scaled the Deployment beyond current node capacity, but no new nodes are being provisioned. The Cluster Autoscaler is deployed and running, but its node group discovery flag references a tag that doesn't match any actual node group — so it sees no groups to scale.
level-8-autoscaler.hint1: Check the Cluster Autoscaler logs to see what node groups it's discovering: `kubectl logs -n k8smissions -l app=cluster-autoscaler | grep -i 'discovery\|group'`
level-8-autoscaler.hint2: The logs should show the autoscaler searching for node groups tagged with `wrong-group` and finding none. Now look at the autoscaler Deployment's `args` to see the full `--node-group-auto-discovery` value.
level-8-autoscaler.hint3: The Cluster Autoscaler's `--node-group-auto-discovery` argument has `asg:tag=wrong-group` but the actual node group uses the tag `kind-worker`. The tag mismatch means the autoscaler discovers zero node groups and cannot add any nodes.
level-8-autoscaler.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Cluster Autoscaler uses the `--node-group-auto-discovery` flag to find node groups by cloud provider tag. The flag is set to `asg:tag=wrong-group` — a tag that doesn't match any existing node group. With no discovered groups, the autoscaler has nothing to scale and pending pods stay pending indefinitely.

## Fix

```yaml
args:
- --node-group-auto-discovery=asg:tag=kind-worker
```

## Why this matters

The Cluster Autoscaler monitors for pods in `Pending` state due to `Insufficient cpu/memory` and tries to add nodes to accommodate them. For this to work, it needs to know which node groups it can scale — discovered via cloud provider tags (AWS ASG tags, GCP MIG labels, etc.). The tag must exactly match what the cloud provider has on the node group. On kind (local), there are no real node groups — this level teaches the configuration pattern even though kind can't actually provision new nodes. In production on EKS/GKE/AKS, a misconfigured discovery tag is the #1 cause of 'autoscaler not scaling' incidents.

level-9-production-runbook.description: A database migration must run before the application can serve traffic safely. The migration Job is failing immediately due to two separate problems: the container image tag doesn't exist and the command always exits with an error code. Both must be fixed before the migration can succeed.
level-9-production-runbook.hint1: Check the status of the migration Job: `kubectl get job db-migration -n k8smissions` and read its logs: `kubectl logs -n k8smissions -l job-name=db-migration`
level-9-production-runbook.hint2: The Job is failing with either an image pull error or an immediate exit code 1. Inspect the Job spec: `kubectl describe job db-migration -n k8smissions`. Look at both the `image` field and the `command` that it runs.
level-9-production-runbook.hint3: The Job has two broken fields: `image: busybox:missing` (a nonexistent tag) and `command: ['/bin/sh', '-c', 'exit 1']` (which always fails). Fix both — use `busybox:1.36` and change the command to something that exits 0.
level-9-production-runbook.reveal:
## What went wrong

The migration Job has `image: busybox:missing` — a tag that doesn't exist — and `command: ['exit 1']` — which always exits with failure code 1. With `restartPolicy: Never`, each failure creates a new pod attempt until `backoffLimit` is reached, at which point the Job enters `Failed` state.

## Fix

```yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: db-migration
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: db-migration
        image: busybox:1.36
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - echo migration complete
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          value: postgres://db/app
```

## Why this matters

The safe production runbook for schema migrations:
1. Scale down the Deployment to 0 replicas (`kubectl scale deployment runbook-app --replicas=0`)
2. Apply and wait for the migration Job (`kubectl wait --for=condition=complete job/db-migration`)
3. Scale the Deployment back up

Skipping step 1 risks the old application code running against a partially migrated schema. Jobs with `restartPolicy: Never` and a reasonable `backoffLimit` (default: 6) are the standard pattern — each attempt gets a fresh pod, and the Job tracks completion vs. failure independently of pod restarts.

level-10-grand-finale.description: Critical production outage. The grand-finale-app Deployment has multiple simultaneous problems: unpinned image tag, only 1 replica, no resource limits, no readiness probe, no graceful shutdown, and a PDB that is mathematically impossible to satisfy. This is a production readiness checklist failure — identify every issue before making any changes.
level-10-grand-finale.hint1: Run a full production readiness review first: `kubectl describe deployment grand-finale-app -n k8smissions` and `kubectl describe pdb grand-finale-pdb -n k8smissions`. List every issue you find before touching the manifest.
level-10-grand-finale.hint2: You should find: image uses `:latest` (unpinned), replicas is 1 (not production-ready), no resource limits, no readinessProbe, no lifecycle preStop hook, no terminationGracePeriodSeconds, and PDB minAvailable: 3 is impossible with only 1 replica.
level-10-grand-finale.hint3: Six things need fixing across two resources: container `image` tag, `spec.replicas`, `resources.limits`, `readinessProbe`, `lifecycle.preStop` + `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` on the Deployment, and `spec.minAvailable` on the PDB.
level-10-grand-finale.reveal:
## What went wrong

Six independent production readiness gaps combined:
1. `image: nginx:latest` — unpinned tag, non-reproducible
2. `replicas: 1` — no redundancy, single point of failure
3. No resource limits — noisy-neighbor risk
4. No readinessProbe — traffic routed to pods that may not be ready
5. No preStop hook — in-flight requests dropped during rollouts
6. `pdb.minAvailable: 3` with `replicas: 1` — drain and rollout permanently blocked

## Fix

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
      containers:
      - name: grand-finale-app
        image: nginx:1.27.4
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 64Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 512Mi
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
        lifecycle:
          preStop:
            exec:
              command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 15"]
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
```

## Why this matters

Production readiness is a checklist, not a single configuration. Every item on this list represents a real class of incident that happens in production: unpinned tags cause surprise breakages, single replicas cause outages during node maintenance, missing limits cause noisy-neighbor events, missing readiness probes route traffic to broken pods, missing preStop causes dropped connections during deploys, and impossible PDBs permanently block cluster maintenance. This level is the synthesis of module 7 — a working system requires all of these properties simultaneously.

level-11-hpa-invalid.description: A HorizontalPodAutoscaler exists for the API Deployment, but the Deployment never scales up or down. The HPA entered an error state at creation time because its configuration contains an impossible constraint — the minimum replica count is set higher than the maximum.
level-11-hpa-invalid.hint1: Inspect the HPA status and conditions: `kubectl describe hpa api-hpa -n k8smissions`
level-11-hpa-invalid.hint2: The status should show an error condition referencing the invalid replica range. Look specifically at the `minReplicas` and `maxReplicas` fields — a valid HPA requires `minReplicas <= maxReplicas`.
level-11-hpa-invalid.hint3: The HPA has `minReplicas: 10` and `maxReplicas: 3`. This is logically impossible — the minimum cannot exceed the maximum. Kubernetes rejects this configuration and the HPA cannot reconcile.
level-11-hpa-invalid.reveal:
## What went wrong

The HPA spec has `minReplicas: 10` and `maxReplicas: 3`. The Kubernetes API server accepts this manifest (it's valid YAML), but the HPA controller cannot reconcile it — you cannot have a minimum higher than a maximum. The HPA enters a permanent error state and the Deployment stays at whatever replica count it was at.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
```

## Why this matters

HPA constraints: `1 <= minReplicas <= maxReplicas`. The HPA controller scales within this range based on the metric target. When CPU utilization exceeds 70%, it scales up; when utilization drops below the target, it scales down (after a cool-down period). A common production mistake is setting `minReplicas` too high and then wondering why costs are elevated — the HPA will never scale below `minReplicas` regardless of how low utilization drops.

level-12-kustomize-wrong-patch.description: A Kustomize overlay was applied and appeared to succeed, but the Deployment replica count never changed. The patch exists, the kustomization.yaml looks correct, and no error was reported. The silent failure is caused by a name mismatch: the patch targets a resource that doesn't exist.
level-12-kustomize-wrong-patch.hint1: Check the actual replica count on the Deployment: `kubectl get deployment -n k8smissions`. Compare it against what the Kustomize patch intended to set.
level-12-kustomize-wrong-patch.hint2: The replica count is still at the base value (1), not the patched value (5). Compare the resource name in the patch `target.name` against the actual name of the Deployment in the cluster — they must match exactly.
level-12-kustomize-wrong-patch.hint3: The patch targets a Deployment named `backend-api` but the actual Deployment is named `api-server`. Kustomize found no resource matching the patch target and silently skipped the patch — no error is reported.
level-12-kustomize-wrong-patch.reveal:
## What went wrong

Kustomize patches use a target selector to find which resource to modify. When the `target.name` doesn't match any resource in the build, Kustomize silently skips the patch — this is a design choice to allow partial overlays, but it creates a hard-to-spot failure mode where patches appear to succeed but have no effect.

## Fix

Change the patch target name to match the actual Deployment:

```yaml
patches:
  - patch: |-
      apiVersion: apps/v1
      kind: Deployment
      metadata:
        name: api-server
      spec:
        replicas: 5
    target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: api-server
```

## Why this matters

Kustomize patch targets match by `kind`, `name`, `namespace`, `labelSelector`, and `annotationSelector`. If any selector field doesn't match, the patch is skipped without error. This silent-skip behavior can cause hours of debugging when a configuration change appears to be applied but has no effect. Always verify with `kubectl kustomize .` (dry-run rendering) before applying, and check the rendered output against your expectation rather than assuming the patch ran.

level-13-helm-values-indent.description: A Helm upgrade was run with a custom values file to change the image tag to `v2.0.0`, but the deployed pods still use the old image. The values file exists and contains the right value — but a YAML indentation error causes `tag` to be parsed as a top-level key instead of a child of `image`.
level-13-helm-values-indent.hint1: Check what image is currently running: `kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep image`. Then look at the values file structure in the manifest, specifically the `image` block.
level-13-helm-values-indent.hint2: The old image tag is still in use. In the broken values YAML, `tag: v2.0.0` appears at the same indentation level as `image:` — not indented under it. YAML uses whitespace as structure, so `tag` becomes a separate root-level key, not `image.tag`.
level-13-helm-values-indent.hint3: The `tag` key in the values file is not indented under `image:`. Both `tag` and `repository` must be indented two spaces under the `image:` key to be parsed as `image.tag` and `image.repository`.
level-13-helm-values-indent.reveal:
## What went wrong

The values file has:
```yaml
image:
tag: v2.0.0       # <-- at root level
  repository: nginx
```

YAML sees `tag` as a root-level key, not `image.tag`. The Helm template `{{ .Values.image.tag }}` finds nothing under `image.tag` and uses its default (the old version).

## Fix

```yaml
image:
  tag: v2.0.0        # indented 2 spaces under image:
  repository: nginx  # also 2 spaces
```

## Why this matters

YAML is whitespace-significant — indentation defines the document hierarchy. A single misaligned key silently parses as a different structure with no YAML error, because `tag: v2.0.0` is valid YAML at the root level. This is one of the most common sources of 'my Helm values aren't being applied' bugs. Always use `helm template . -f values.yaml` to render the chart locally and verify the output before running `helm upgrade`.

level-14-argocd-selfheal.description: A Deployment was manually scaled via `kubectl scale` to respond to a sudden traffic spike, but ArgoCD reverted it back to the Git value within seconds. Every manual `kubectl` change disappears moments after it's applied. ArgoCD is behaving exactly as configured — `selfHeal: true` is working as designed, which is the problem.
level-14-argocd-selfheal.hint1: Read the ArgoCD Application's sync policy to understand the behavior: `kubectl get application my-app -n argocd -o yaml | grep -A10 syncPolicy`
level-14-argocd-selfheal.hint2: You should see `selfHeal: true` under `automated`. Self-healing means ArgoCD continuously compares live cluster state to Git and immediately reverts any drift — including your intentional manual changes.
level-14-argocd-selfheal.hint3: The Application's `syncPolicy.automated.selfHeal` is `true`. This causes ArgoCD to detect any deviation from Git (including your scale command) as drift and auto-revert it within its next sync cycle (typically seconds to minutes). Set it to `false` to allow manual overrides to persist.
level-14-argocd-selfheal.reveal:
## What went wrong

`selfHeal: true` is a deliberate GitOps enforcement feature — it ensures the cluster always matches Git, even if someone runs `kubectl` directly. This is usually desirable. But when you need a temporary manual override (emergency scale-up during an incident), self-healing fights you.

## Fix

```yaml
syncPolicy:
  automated:
    selfHeal: false
    prune: true
```

## Why this matters

This level illustrates the core GitOps tension: Git is the source of truth, but emergencies sometimes require immediate manual action. Three approaches:
1. **Disable self-heal** (this fix) — allows manual overrides but permits drift
2. **Update Git first** — make the change in Git, ArgoCD applies it; purist GitOps, but slower
3. **Suspend the Application** — `argocd app set my-app --sync-policy none` temporarily

For production incidents, option 3 is often best: suspend ArgoCD, apply the emergency fix manually, then commit the change to Git and re-enable sync. This preserves the GitOps audit trail while unblocking the incident.

level-15-namespace-stuck.description: A namespace has been stuck in Terminating state for over an hour. `kubectl delete namespace zombie-ns` completed without error but the namespace never disappeared. A ConfigMap inside holds a finalizer registered by a controller that was deleted — and since no controller will ever clear that finalizer, the namespace is stuck forever.
level-15-namespace-stuck.hint1: Find what's blocking deletion inside the namespace: `kubectl get all,configmap -n zombie-ns` and look for resources with finalizers: `kubectl get configmap stuck-resource -n zombie-ns -o yaml | grep -A5 finalizers`
level-15-namespace-stuck.hint2: You should find `finalizers: [some.controller.io/cleanup]` on the ConfigMap. This finalizer was registered by a controller that has since been deleted. Without the controller running, the finalizer will never be removed — it must be force-cleared manually.
level-15-namespace-stuck.hint3: The ConfigMap `stuck-resource` has a finalizer `some.controller.io/cleanup` that will never be cleared automatically. You need to patch the ConfigMap to set `finalizers: []`, which removes the block and allows the namespace deletion to complete.
level-15-namespace-stuck.reveal:
## What went wrong

Finalizers are strings added to a resource's metadata that block deletion until they are removed. The controller that registered `some.controller.io/cleanup` was deleted without first clearing the finalizer from the ConfigMap. Kubernetes waits forever for the finalizer to be cleared — which can never happen now.

## Fix

Force-remove the finalizer from the ConfigMap:

```bash
kubectl patch configmap stuck-resource -n zombie-ns \
  --type=json \
  -p='[{"op":"remove","path":"/metadata/finalizers"}]'
```

Or via the manifest, set:

```yaml
metadata:
  finalizers: []
```

Once all resource finalizers are cleared, the namespace finalizer clears automatically and the namespace deletion completes.

## Why this matters

Finalizers are Kubernetes' mechanism for ordered cleanup: operators register a finalizer on resources they manage, do their cleanup work when deletion is requested, then remove the finalizer to allow deletion to proceed. The failure mode is when the operator is deleted without cleaning up its finalizers first — leaving orphaned finalizers that block deletion permanently. Always check `metadata.finalizers` when a resource refuses to delete. The nuclear option (force-deleting a namespace via the API server proxy) bypasses finalizers entirely but can leave orphaned resources in etcd.
